{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.9}}

Summary: Tightened the prose and, more importantly, surfaced a contradiction the original buries — the method claims to avoid ensembles but trains its confidence head against a K=10 bootstrap-ensemble target.

The central problem
--------------------
The section asserts (paragraph 1) that VerbalQ obtains calibrated uncertainty "without resorting to ensembles of value functions or to bootstrap-head disagreement," and then (paragraph 2) trains the confidence head against the empirical variance across K=10 bootstrap heads. Ensembles are required at training time; only inference is single-pass. The current wording elides this. Any honest framing has to make the training/inference asymmetry explicit, otherwise reviewers will (correctly) call this overclaiming.

A related issue: the deployment-efficiency claim is described as what "distinguishes VerbalQ from ensemble-based uncertainty methods." It only distinguishes VerbalQ *at inference*. Bootstrapped DQN and similar methods also incur their cost mainly at training; if your inference baseline is also single-network, the gap shrinks. The comparison needs to specify which axis of compute.

Other concrete issues
---------------------
1. "Matches Bootstrapped DQN on Montezuma's Revenge and Pitfall" is two games out of Atari-57; the K=10 sufficiency claim invokes "all Atari-57 environments." Either report aggregate Atari-57 numbers or scope both claims to the hard-exploration subset.
2. The mapping from variance to token is under-specified: "normalized variance" by what normalizer, and is the token assignment done per-state or per-batch? This matters for whether the cross-entropy target is stable.
3. The verbal tags ("low/medium/high") are introduced as natural language but then immediately decoded to scalars. The "natural-language" framing adds nothing technically and invites questions about why a 3-way categorical needs the linguistic dressing. Either justify it (interpretability for human operators? logging?) or drop it and call it a discretized confidence head.
4. Paragraph 3 ("Exploration is then driven by...") restates paragraph 1. Fold it in.

Revised text
------------
We introduce VerbalQ, a deep reinforcement-learning framework in which the agent emits a discrete confidence token (one of "low", "medium", "high") alongside each action-value estimate. The token is mapped to a scalar probability and used to modulate exploration: states with low expressed confidence receive larger exploration bonuses. The design goal is a value network that, *at inference*, produces calibrated uncertainty from a single forward pass — no ensemble evaluation, no bootstrap-head disagreement computation, no auxiliary structure on the deployed agent.

The training procedure does, however, use a bootstrap ensemble as a teacher. The policy network has a shared trunk feeding three heads: an action-value head, a confidence head, and K=10 bootstrap value heads used only during training. The action-value head is trained with the standard TD(0) loss against a target network. The confidence head is trained with a cross-entropy objective against a target token derived from the empirical variance across the K=10 bootstrap heads, with the target token chosen as the one whose induced probability is closest to 1 − v̂, where v̂ is the per-state variance normalized by [specify normalizer]. We find K=10 sufficient to stabilize the confidence-head signal across the hard-exploration subset of Atari-57 we evaluate on. At inference the bootstrap heads are discarded, and exploration is driven entirely by the predicted token.

This training/inference asymmetry is the basis of VerbalQ's efficiency claim. Against Bootstrapped DQN, training cost is comparable — both maintain K=10 heads — but inference cost drops to that of a single-network DQN. On Montezuma's Revenge and Pitfall, VerbalQ matches Bootstrapped DQN's return while using [X×] less inference compute per step. Aggregate Atari-57 results are reported in Section [N].

Uncertainty disclosure
----------------------
The revised numbers ("[X×]", "Section [N]", "[specify normalizer]") are placeholders — I don't have access to the underlying experiments. The structural critique (the ensemble-at-training contradiction, the scope mismatch between Montezuma/Pitfall and "all Atari-57") stands independent of those numbers.
